items.q: What are conventions of the Gothic genre?
items.correct: Settings of ruin and decay, supernatural or psychological terror, transgression of boundaries, the sublime, doubled or haunted protagonists, and atmospheric excess.
items.misconception: Gothic just means scary stories with vampires and ghosts.
items.random_wrong: Gothic refers to medieval architecture, not literature.
items.why: Students reduce Gothic to monsters. The genre is defined by its concerns — boundaries, the past intruding on the present, the limits of reason — and only sometimes uses supernatural creatures.

items.correct: Their account is undermined within the text by ignorance, bias, self-deception or deceit, so the reader must read against what they say.
items.misconception: An unreliable narrator is one who tells lies in the story.
items.random_wrong: An unreliable narrator is when the author makes mistakes.
items.why: Unreliability is structural — the text gives signals the narrator is wrong (e.g. internal contradictions). Lying intentionally is one kind, but blind spots and self-deception count too.

items.correct: A line of five iambs (unstressed-stressed syllable pairs), creating a ten-syllable line with the rhythm da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM.
items.misconception: Iambic pentameter is any line with ten syllables.
items.random_wrong: Iambic pentameter is when every line rhymes.
items.why: Syllable count alone doesn't make iambic pentameter — the stress pattern is essential. And iambic pentameter is usually unrhymed (blank verse) in Shakespeare's plays.

items.q: What is Aristotle's term for tragic flaw?
items.why: It's Hamartia. “Catharsis” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Reconciliation. “Death” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many key plot components does Aristotle's tragic arc identify (hamartia, peripeteia, anagnorisis, catharsis)?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
